data.title: China High Precision Automation Group (HKG:591) Seems To Use Debt Rather Sparingly
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' So it seems the smart money knows that debt - which is usually involved in bankruptcies - is a very important factor, when you assess how risky a company is. Importantly, China High Precision Automation Group Limited (HKG:591) does carry debt. But the real question is whether this debt is making the company risky.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Why Does Debt Bring Risk?

Debt and other liabilities become risky for a business when it cannot easily fulfill those obligations, either with free cash flow or by raising capital at an attractive price. In the worst case scenario, a company can go bankrupt if it cannot pay its creditors. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, the upside of debt is that it often represents cheap capital, especially when it replaces dilution in a company with the ability to reinvest at high rates of return. The first step when considering a company's debt levels is to consider its cash and debt together.

What Is China High Precision Automation Group's Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at June 2025 China High Precision Automation Group had debt of CN¥18.0m, up from CN¥13.9m in one year. However, its balance sheet shows it holds CN¥1.46b in cash, so it actually has CN¥1.44b net cash.

How Strong Is China High Precision Automation Group's Balance Sheet?

Zooming in on the latest balance sheet data, we can see that China High Precision Automation Group had liabilities of CN¥77.7m due within 12 months and liabilities of CN¥18.1m due beyond that. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of CN¥1.46b as well as receivables valued at CN¥77.7m due within 12 months. So it can boast CN¥1.44b more liquid assets than total liabilities.

This surplus liquidity suggests that China High Precision Automation Group's balance sheet could take a hit just as well as Homer Simpson's head can take a punch. With this in mind one could posit that its balance sheet means the company is able to handle some adversity. Succinctly put, China High Precision Automation Group boasts net cash, so it's fair to say it does not have a heavy debt load!

View our latest analysis for China High Precision Automation Group

It was also good to see that despite losing money on the EBIT line last year, China High Precision Automation Group turned things around in the last 12 months, delivering and EBIT of CN¥16m. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But you can't view debt in total isolation; since China High Precision Automation Group will need earnings to service that debt. So if you're keen to discover more about its earnings, it might be worth checking out this graph of its long term earnings trend.

Finally, a company can only pay off debt with cold hard cash, not accounting profits. China High Precision Automation Group may have net cash on the balance sheet, but it is still interesting to look at how well the business converts its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) to free cash flow, because that will influence both its need for, and its capacity to manage debt. Over the last year, China High Precision Automation Group actually produced more free cash flow than EBIT. There's nothing better than incoming cash when it comes to staying in your lenders' good graces.

Summing Up

While it is always sensible to investigate a company's debt, in this case China High Precision Automation Group has CN¥1.44b in net cash and a strong balance sheet. And it impressed us with free cash flow of CN¥33m, being 211% of its EBIT. So is China High Precision Automation Group's debt a risk? It doesn't seem so to us. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. We've identified 2 warning signs with China High Precision Automation Group , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

If, after all that, you're more interested in a fast growing company with a rock-solid balance sheet, then check out our list of net cash growth stocks without delay.

Approach 1: Diageo Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s intrinsic value by forecasting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s dollars. This gives a clear sense of what Diageo might be worth based on the cash it can generate in the years ahead.

Currently, Diageo’s last twelve months of Free Cash Flow stand at $2.79 Billion. Analyst estimates project this figure rising steadily, with $3.75 Billion expected for 2028. While professional analyst forecasts cover about five years, projections for the following five years are extrapolated from trends, with Free Cash Flow climbing towards $4.78 Billion by 2035.

The DCF analysis implies a fair value of $30.25 per share for Diageo compared to the stock’s present trading price. This represents a 39.9% intrinsic discount, which indicates the stock is undervalued according to this model.

Approach 2: Diageo Price vs Earnings (PE)

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely used valuation tool for profitable companies like Diageo, as it tells investors how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings. It is generally considered a good indicator when evaluating established businesses with a strong track record of profitability. A higher PE ratio can signal that investors expect future earnings growth, while a lower one may reflect lower growth prospects or higher risk.

Currently, Diageo’s PE ratio sits at 22.7x. To put this in context, this is above the broader Beverage industry average of 18.3x, and also higher than the average among peers, which is 20.7x. On the surface, this suggests Diageo is priced at a premium compared to its competitors. However, context matters: growth expectations, profit stability, and market risks all play a role in what a fair PE should be for any company.

That is where Simply Wall St's proprietary "Fair Ratio" comes in. For Diageo, the Fair Ratio is calculated at 26.7x, which takes into account not just the company’s growth profile and industry positioning, but also unique risk factors, margins, and its scale. Unlike just comparing with industry or peer multiples, the Fair Ratio offers a more tailored perspective on what is reasonable for Diageo specifically, making it particularly useful for investors looking beyond simple benchmarks.

Comparing Diageo’s current PE of 22.7x to its Fair Ratio of 26.7x indicates that the shares are trading below what would be considered fair based on the company’s fundamentals. This points to Diageo being undervalued on a PE basis, despite appearing expensive against simpler averages.

data.title: Will Shanghai Electric Group’s (SEHK:2727) Fusion Milestone Redefine Its Global Clean Energy Ambitions?
data.description:

  • These breakthroughs highlight China’s expanding role in the global fusion supply chain and set new international engineering benchmarks for manufacturing ultra-large core components.

For anyone considering Shanghai Electric Group, the big picture centers on the company’s ambition to be a leader in global clean energy supply chains. The recent breakthrough in delivering critical nuclear fusion components to the ITER project is a tangible sign of engineering capability. However, the immediate impact on most key short-term catalysts, such as revenue, earnings momentum, or market sentiment, may not be material at this stage, as the commercial payoff for fusion remains long term. Instead, the announcement shines a spotlight on Shanghai Electric’s long-term position in advanced energy, which could gradually shift risk perceptions if further similar milestones are reached. Still, with high price-to-earnings ratios, a rapidly changing board, and slow revenue growth compared to market peers, some of the biggest risks around execution, governance, and valuation remain just as relevant following this news event.
